title: Test, monitor, and troubleshoot
content:
Test, Monitor, and Troubleshoot After the frontend, API Gateway, and backend are deployed, validate the whole system and check operational logs.
Test 1: Backend Health Check each layer:
http://<elastic-beanstalk-domain>/api/health https://<api-gateway-endpoint>/api/health https://<amplify-domain>/api/health Expected response:
{ "success": true, "message": "OK", "data": { "status": "ok" } } If it fails, check in this order: Elastic Beanstalk logs, API Gateway route/integration/stage, then the Amplify rewrite rule.
Test 2: Admin Login Open the Amplify URL and sign in with the seeded admin account.
Figure 5.6.1. Login page on the Amplify domain.
The frontend should open from the Amplify domain and the login form should display correctly. This is the starting point for user workflow testing.
Expected results:
User is redirected to /admin/dashboard. Dashboard metrics are displayed. Sidebar and navigation work. API calls return 200 or the expected business response. Figure 5.6.2. Admin dashboard after successful login.
After admin login, the dashboard should display summary data. This confirms that authentication, backend APIs, and the database are working together.
Test 3: Main Admin Workflows Test at least one workflow from each major admin module:
Module Test Action Categories Create or search asset categories. Assets Create, update, or search assets. Employees View the employee list and employee details. Departments View or update department information. Assignments Assign an available asset to an employee. Maintenance Create or update a support/maintenance request. Inventory View or create an inventory session. Reports Open summary or asset reports. Figure 5.6.3. Asset management screen.
On the asset management screen, check that the asset list loads correctly and that core actions such as view, edit, delete, or Excel import appear according to admin permissions.
Figure 5.6.4. Asset assignment workflow.
In the assignment workflow, verify asset status, assigned employee, and assignment history. This is a key business workflow in EAM Workspace.
Test 4: Employee Workflows Sign in with an employee account and check:
Employee dashboard. Assigned assets page. Asset detail page. Support request creation. FAQ page. Profile and history pages. Figure 5.6.5. Employee dashboard after sign-in.
On the employee dashboard, verify that data is displayed according to employee permissions. The user should only see assigned assets, support requests, and personal information related to their account.
Test 5: Upload and Account Status Also test the cases encountered during deployment:
Image/avatar upload goes through backend/API and the image returns 200. Inactive accounts cannot sign in. Inactive account errors return the expected business code, for example AUTH_ACCOUNT_INACTIVE. Browser DevTools shows no CORS errors. Figure 5.6.6. DevTools Network showing successful API requests.
In the Network tab, filter /api/... requests and check that the status is 200 or an expected business response. If CORS errors or HTML responses appear, recheck Amplify rewrites and backend CORS settings.
Figure 5.6.7. Inactive account blocked from signing in.
When signing in with an inactive account, the system should deny access. This confirms that the backend checks account status before granting system access.
Monitoring with CloudWatch Open CloudWatch Logs and check the Elastic Beanstalk log group.
Check:
Backend startup logs. Request logs. Authentication errors. Database connection errors. Unhandled exceptions. Useful endpoints:
GET /api/health POST /api/auth/login GET /api/assets GET /api/reports/summary Suggested Alarms For a demo environment, simple CloudWatch alarms can be created:
Alarm Purpose EC2 high CPU Detect backend instance overload. Elastic Beanstalk health degraded Detect unstable backend environments. RDS CPU or storage Detect database capacity issues. 5xx errors Detect application or infrastructure errors. Troubleshooting Guide Issue Common Cause Fix 502 or backend not responding Backend crash or wrong port Check PORT=8080, EB logs, and direct health endpoint. API Gateway returns 404 Wrong route, stage, or integration Check ANY /{proxy+}, integration endpoint, deployed stage, and path forwarding. CORS error Wrong FRONTEND_ORIGIN or FRONTEND_ORIGINS Set the correct Amplify URL and redeploy backend. Cannot connect to database Wrong RDS endpoint or security group Check DATABASE_URL and allow 3306 from backend SG. /api/... returns frontend HTML Amplify rewrite order is wrong Move /api/<*> above the SPA fallback rule. Static JS/CSS MIME type error Rewrite rule captures static assets incorrectly Rewrite only /api/<*> to API Gateway and let SPA fallback handle frontend routes. Login fails Seed data or JWT config issue Check seed data, JWT_SECRET, and backend logs. Upload is not durable Local instance storage is being used Move upload storage to S3 for production-ready deployment. Validation Checklist Frontend opens from the Amplify URL. GET /api/health succeeds through Elastic Beanstalk. GET /api/health succeeds through API Gateway. GET /api/health succeeds through Amplify rewrite. Admin login works. Employee login works. Main CRUD workflows work. Asset assignment workflow works. Support request workflow works. Inactive accounts are blocked from signing in. Browser DevTools shows no CORS errors.

title: Clean up resources
content:
Clean Up Resources After completing the workshop for deploying EAM Workspace on AWS, unused resources should be cleaned up to avoid unexpected AWS charges. In this workshop, the main resources include AWS Amplify Hosting, Amazon API Gateway, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Amazon RDS for MySQL, Amazon SES, security groups, and related logs/metrics.
Before deleting resources, capture the important screens for the report. These screenshots show that the project was deployed, tested, and has a clear cleanup plan.
Workshop Summary In this workshop, EAM Workspace was deployed as a full-stack web application on AWS:
The React/Vite frontend was built and hosted with AWS Amplify Hosting. Frontend /api/* requests were routed to Amazon API Gateway. API Gateway forwarded requests to the backend running on AWS Elastic Beanstalk. The backend connected to Amazon RDS for MySQL for business data. Email/OTP functionality used Amazon SES through SMTP credentials. Deployment results were validated with the health endpoint, login page, dashboard, and key business screens. After testing and capturing screenshots, the demo resources can be cleaned up in the order below.
Step 1: Record Resources To Clean Up Before deleting anything, record the names of resources created during the workshop:
Resource Group Name or Item To Check Amplify app Frontend app quanlidoanhnghiep or deployment branch aws-architecture API Gateway HTTP API eam-backend-http-api Elastic Beanstalk Backend environment, for example eam-backend-prod-v8 RDS MySQL database used by EAM Workspace SES Email identity and SMTP credential used for email CloudWatch Logs/metrics related to Elastic Beanstalk or API EC2/Security Group Security groups for Elastic Beanstalk and RDS This list helps avoid deleting resources that do not belong to the workshop.
Step 2: Delete or Disconnect the AWS Amplify App AWS Amplify Hosting is used to build and publish the frontend. If the demo frontend is no longer needed, the app can be deleted or the deployment branch can be disconnected.
Steps:
Open the AWS Amplify console. Select the frontend app, for example quanlidoanhnghiep. Select the deployment branch, for example aws-architecture. Capture the app/branch screen before deletion. If a full cleanup is required, go to App settings or General settings and delete the app. Confirm that the Amplify domain is no longer used for the demo. If the frontend is still needed for presentation, Amplify can be kept temporarily, but this should be noted in the report.
Figure 5.7.1. AWS Amplify app quanlidoanhnghiep and branch aws-architecture before cleanup. This screenshot records that the frontend was deployed successfully, had an active Amplify domain, and should be handled if the demo is no longer maintained.
After capturing this screenshot, clean up Amplify as follows:
If the demo frontend is no longer needed, go to App settings -> General settings -> Delete app. If only the deployment branch should be removed, select the aws-architecture branch and disconnect/delete it. Check the Amplify domain again after deletion to confirm the public URL is no longer available. If the app is kept for report presentation, note that Amplify is still active and continue monitoring its cost in Billing. Step 3: Delete Amazon API Gateway API Gateway is the public endpoint layer used by the frontend to call the backend. If the API is no longer used, delete it so the endpoint no longer receives requests.
Steps:
Open the Amazon API Gateway console. Select the project HTTP API, for example eam-backend-http-api. Check the ANY /{proxy+} route and the integration pointing to the Elastic Beanstalk backend. Capture the API list or route/integration page. Choose Delete if the API is no longer needed. Check the API list again to confirm deletion. After API Gateway is deleted, URLs in the form https://...execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/... will no longer work.
Figure 5.7.2. HTTP API eam-backend-http-api in Amazon API Gateway before deletion. This screen confirms the correct public backend API endpoint and helps avoid deleting an API that does not belong to the project.
After capturing the API screenshot, clean it up as follows:
Select the correct HTTP API eam-backend-http-api. Recheck the ANY /{proxy+} route and its integration with the Elastic Beanstalk backend. If the frontend/backend demo is no longer used, choose Delete to remove the API. After deletion, test the API endpoint again or check the API list to confirm it no longer exists. If Amplify is still kept, update the rewrite configuration or note that /api/* requests will no longer work. Step 4: Terminate the Elastic Beanstalk Environment Elastic Beanstalk manages the Node.js/Express backend environment. It may create EC2 instances, load balancers, Auto Scaling resources, and security groups, so it should be terminated when no longer used.
Steps:
Open the Elastic Beanstalk console. Select the backend environment, for example eam-backend-prod-v8. Capture the environment screen before termination, including health, platform, and domain. Choose Actions -> Terminate environment. Enter the environment name if AWS asks for confirmation. Wait until the environment is fully terminated. Check the EC2 console to make sure instances created by Elastic Beanstalk are stopped/deleted. If Elastic Beanstalk cannot terminate because of dependencies, check related security groups, load balancers, or EC2 resources.
Figure 5.7.3. Elastic Beanstalk environment eam-backend-prod-v8 before termination. The screenshot shows that the backend environment was healthy, had its own domain, and could continue generating cost if left running.
To clean up the Elastic Beanstalk backend:
Open the eam-backend-prod-v8 environment. Choose Actions -> Terminate environment. Enter the exact environment name if AWS asks for confirmation. Wait until the environment is terminated, then check EC2 to confirm the related instances/load balancers were removed. If termination fails because of dependencies, check related load balancers, Auto Scaling groups, network interfaces, and security groups. Step 5: Delete or Stop Amazon RDS for MySQL Amazon RDS is an important resource because database instances can continue to generate cost while running. Before deleting it, decide whether the demo data still needs to be kept.
Steps:
Open the Amazon RDS console. Select the MySQL database used by EAM Workspace. Capture the database list or database detail screen. Create a snapshot first if the data must be kept. If the data is no longer needed, choose Delete. For a demo environment, a final snapshot can be skipped if the data is not required. Confirm that the database changes to deleting/deleted status. If the RDS database is not deleted immediately, stop it if appropriate and continue monitoring cost in Billing.
Figure 5.7.4. Amazon RDS database eam-mysql before cleanup. This resource needs careful attention because a database instance can still incur cost while it remains available.
For RDS, cleanup should be done carefully because data may be lost:
If demo data must be kept, create a snapshot before deleting the database. If the data is no longer required, select eam-mysql -> Actions -> Delete. For a demo environment, the final snapshot can be skipped only when recovery is not needed. Confirm that the database changes to deleting/deleted status. After RDS is deleted, check related security groups because they may no longer have dependencies and can be removed. Step 6: Check Amazon SES Amazon SES is used to verify an email identity and create SMTP credentials for email/OTP. SES usually does not generate large costs if email volume is low, but the configuration should still be reviewed.
Steps:
Open the Amazon SES console. Check the verified email identity. Check the SMTP credential/IAM user created for SES. If it is no longer used, delete the IAM user or credential related to SMTP. Do not include SMTP passwords in screenshots or the report. If SES is kept for further demo, store credentials carefully and do not commit them to GitHub.
Figure 5.7.5. Verified Amazon SES email identity in the Singapore region. This screenshot records the email/OTP sending configuration for the backend while keeping SMTP credentials out of the report.
For SES, cleanup focuses on the identity and credentials:
If email/OTP sending is no longer needed, delete the email identity in Amazon SES. Open IAM and find the SMTP user/credential created for SES. Delete the access key or IAM user if it is no longer used. Recheck Elastic Beanstalk environment variables and local .env files so MAIL_USER, MAIL_PASSWORD, or SMTP secrets are not committed to GitHub. Do not capture or include SMTP passwords in the report. Step 7: Delete Security Groups and Dependent Resources After Elastic Beanstalk and RDS are deleted, check security groups that are no longer used.
Steps:
Open the EC2 console. Go to Security Groups. Find security groups related to the Elastic Beanstalk backend and RDS. Delete a security group only when no resources depend on it. If AWS reports a dependency error, check EC2 instances, load balancers, RDS, or network interfaces. Do not delete default security groups or security groups that are not part of the workshop.
Step 8: Check CloudWatch and Billing After the main resources are cleaned up, check logs and costs.
Steps:
Open the CloudWatch console. Check log groups related to Elastic Beanstalk or the backend. Delete unused log groups or reduce retention if logs should be kept. Open Billing and Cost Management. Check Cost Explorer or Bills to confirm there are no unexpected costs. The resources that should be checked most carefully for cost are RDS databases, EC2/Elastic Beanstalk, load balancers, NAT Gateways, Elastic IPs, and CloudWatch Logs.
Figure 5.7.6. Billing and Cost Management used to review costs after the AWS deployment. The Cost summary and Cost breakdown help check charges by service, such as RDS, EC2, VPC, and Amplify.
After cleaning up the main resources, verify cost status:
Open Billing and Cost Management. Check Cost summary for the current month cost and end-of-month forecast. Review Cost breakdown by service to identify any service that is still generating charges. Pay special attention to Amazon RDS, EC2, Elastic Load Balancing, VPC, API Gateway, Amplify, and CloudWatch. If the cost still increases unexpectedly after cleanup, return to each service and check for remaining resources. Final Cleanup Checklist Resource list recorded. Amplify app/branch screenshot captured before deletion or disconnect. Unused API Gateway deleted. Elastic Beanstalk environment terminated. RDS database deleted or stopped according to demo needs. SES identity and SMTP credential checked. Security groups without dependencies deleted. CloudWatch log groups checked. Billing/Cost Explorer checked after cleanup.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Here, you can freely share your personal opinions about your experience participating in the First Cloud AI Journey program. This will help the FCAJ team improve any shortcomings based on the following aspects:
Overall Evaluation 1. Working Environment I evaluate the working environment of the program as very professional, friendly, and motivating. Throughout my participation, I always received support from mentors and team members whenever I encountered difficulties. The open working atmosphere helped me confidently express my ideas and take more initiative in my studies.
2. Support from Mentor / Team Admin Mentors always provided dedicated guidance, answered my questions, and offered constructive feedback to help me improve my knowledge and skills. The admin team was also prompt in providing information, materials, and resolving issues that arose, ensuring my participation in the program went smoothly.
3. Relevance of Work to Academic Major The program content aligned well with my Information Technology major. I had the opportunity to learn about cloud computing, AI, and real-world project development processes, applying what I learned to practical execution while acquiring new insights.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities The program offered numerous opportunities to develop both my professional knowledge and soft skills. I learned how to work effectively in a team, manage time, conduct research, solve problems, and access AWS services through hands-on practical exercises.
5. Culture & Team Spirit I was highly impressed by the supportive and collaborative spirit among the program participants. Everyone was always willing to share knowledge and discuss solutions to difficulties, creating a positive learning environment and making me feel comfortable throughout my participation.
6. Internship Policies / Benefits Although the main goal of the program is training and skill development, I highly value the knowledge-sharing sessions, workshops, and study materials provided. These are practical values that helped me enhance my professional capabilities.
Additional Questions What did you find most satisfying during your internship?
What I found most satisfying was learning from highly experienced mentors and gaining exposure to real-world knowledge of AWS, Cloud, and AI. These sharing sessions helped clarify my career path and improve my technical skills. What do you think the program should improve for future interns?
In my opinion, the program could organize more sharing sessions with alumni or seniors working in tech companies. Additionally, increasing networking activities between teams would give everyone more opportunities to learn and connect. If recommending to a friend, would you suggest they participate in the program? Why or why not?
Yes. I would recommend it because this program helps students access real-world knowledge of Cloud and AI, learning from experienced mentors in a professional environment. This is an excellent opportunity to prepare before entering the professional workforce after graduation. Suggestions & Expectations Do you have any suggestions to improve the internship experience?
I hope the program will offer more practice sessions based on real-world scenarios, specialized workshops on AWS and AI, and more networking activities among participants to increase opportunities for experience exchange and teamwork. Would you like to continue this program in the future?
Yes. If the program continues to organize advanced courses or activities, I would love the opportunity to participate to update my knowledge and develop my professional skills. Any other comments (free sharing):
I would like to express my gratitude to the entire FCAJ team, the mentors, and the admin team who have always supported me dedicatedly during my participation in the program. This has been a very memorable experience that helped me grow in knowledge, skills, and working mindset. I hope the program will continue to develop and bring high-quality learning opportunities to students in future cohorts.
